metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hashicorp/random` (with a pinned `~> 3.x` version constraint) to `required_providers` in `infra/terraform/versions.tf` and run `terraform init` to update `.terraform.lock.hcl`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `random_password` resources in `auth_sso.tf` but never specifies adding `hashicorp/random` to `required_providers` in `versions.tf`. The lock file confirms only `hashicorp/aws` is currently declared. Without this provider block, `terraform validate` (AC3) will fail with 'provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/random: required by this configuration but no version constraint' or an implicit install that breaks the locked-provider guarantee.
findings.location: What section — auth_sso.tf bullet; AC: `terraform validate` passes
findings.suggested_fix:
In `infra/terraform/versions.tf`, inside `required_providers`, add:
```hcl
random = {
  source  = "hashicorp/random"
  version = "~> 3.6"
}
```
Then run `terraform init -upgrade` to add the lock-file entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hashicorp/archive` (e.g. `~> 2.0`) to `required_providers` in `infra/terraform/versions.tf` and update `.terraform.lock.hcl` via `terraform init`.
findings.finding: The plan relies on `archive_file` data source in `auth_host.tf` (to package the Lambda zip from the copied `index.js`/`cookie.js`) but never mentions declaring the `hashicorp/archive` provider. The lock file confirms it is absent. Without it, `terraform validate` fails.
findings.location: What section — auth_host.tf bullet ('Copy Lambda source … needed by `archive_file`'); AC3
findings.suggested_fix:
In `infra/terraform/versions.tf`, inside `required_providers`, add:
```hcl
archive = {
  source  = "hashicorp/archive"
  version = "~> 2.7"
}
```
Then run `terraform init -upgrade`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hashicorp/random` and `hashicorp/archive` to `required_providers` in `versions.tf` (and run `terraform init` to update the lock file) before adding `random_password` or `archive_file` resources.
findings.finding: The plan adds `random_password` (requires `hashicorp/random`) and `archive_file` (requires `hashicorp/archive`) resources, but `versions.tf` currently only declares `hashicorp/aws`. Neither provider is mentioned anywhere in the plan. `terraform init` will error and `terraform validate` (AC #3) will fail until both providers are declared in `required_providers`.
findings.location: What section — auth_sso.tf (`random_password`), auth_host.tf (`archive_file`); infra/terraform/versions.tf

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hashicorp/random` (version `~> 3.3` or higher, which introduced `random_password` import support) and `hashicorp/archive` to `required_providers` in `versions.tf`, then run `terraform init` to update `.terraform.lock.hcl`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `random_password` (auth_sso.tf) and `archive_file` (auth_host.tf Lambda packaging) resources, which require `hashicorp/random` and `hashicorp/archive` providers respectively. However, `versions.tf` only declares `hashicorp/aws`. These providers are not in `required_providers`, so `terraform init` will either fail or pull an unconstrained/unverified version. The plan makes no mention of adding these provider declarations.
findings.location: `What` section — `auth_sso.tf`: `random_password`; `auth_host.tf`: `archive_file`; `infra/terraform/versions.tf`
findings.suggested_fix: In versions.tf required_providers block, add: `random = { source = "hashicorp/random", version = "~> 3.5" }` and `archive = { source = "hashicorp/archive", version = "~> 2.0" }`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement the 'no secret-value change' evidence with a command that can actually prove it, such as capturing the SSM parameter value hash before and after apply (`aws ssm get-parameter --with-decryption | sha256sum`) and confirming they match, or using `terraform show -json` to compare sensitive-value metadata.
findings.finding: AC4's 'no secret-value change' sub-criterion is unverifiable from `terraform plan` output alone: Terraform renders all `sensitive` values as `(sensitive)` in plan output, so a plan comment cannot distinguish 'value unchanged' from 'value silently replaced'. An executor cannot produce evidence of no-secret-change by posting plan output.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'terraform plan shows no destroy/replace and no secret-value change (evidence: plan output as a comment)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre/post comparison step: before import, record `aws ssm get-parameter --name <cookie_signing_key_path> --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value | sha256sum` and after apply confirm the hash is identical. Record both hashes as the AC evidence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hashicorp/random` and `hashicorp/archive` to `required_providers` in `infra/terraform/versions.tf` and regenerate `.terraform.lock.hcl` via `terraform init`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `random_password` (requires `hashicorp/random`) and `archive_file` (requires `hashicorp/archive`) resources/data-sources, but `versions.tf` currently only declares `hashicorp/aws`, and `.terraform.lock.hcl` contains only the aws provider hash. Neither provider is declared as a `required_providers` entry. Without these declarations, `terraform init` will fail to download the providers and `terraform validate` (AC #3) cannot pass.
findings.location: What section (auth_sso.tf / auth_host.tf bullets) + infra/terraform/versions.tf + infra/terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an [operator-attested] AC requiring a post-apply smoke-test of the auth_host endpoint (e.g., `curl -I https://auth.solutions.navateam.com/` returns HTTP 200/302 with a `Set-Cookie` header, or equivalent) to confirm the SSO stack is live and signing cookies correctly after the state migration.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the live auth_host SSO endpoint end-to-end after import+apply. The plan migrates a live, user-facing SSO stack (Lambda + CloudFront + Route53 + API GW) but the operator-attested ACs only verify `terraform plan` shows no destroy/replace — they do not require a smoke-test of the actual SSO flow (e.g., hitting the auth_host URL and confirming a valid cookie-signed redirect still works). A clean plan does not prove the Lambda code, env vars, or origin-secret wiring survived the state migration intact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 and AC5 (operator-attested)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Acceptance Criteria:
```
- [ ] [operator-attested] Post-apply smoke-test: `curl -sI https://<auth_host_fqdn>/` returns HTTP 2xx/3xx with a Set-Cookie or Location header (evidence: curl output recorded as a comment), confirming the SSO Lambda, API GW, and CloudFront wiring are intact.
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out procedure for the `terraform state rm` step (e.g., 're-import to snap state if rebar apply fails', or sequence state-rm only after a successful targeted apply of each resource), especially since auth_host serves live traffic.
findings.finding: The `terraform state rm` from snap's shared state is an irreversible operation executed against live-traffic resources (auth_host Lambda/API GW/CloudFront serve active SSO), but the plan provides no rollback or back-out path if the rebar apply fails after the state-rm is issued.
findings.location: What (last bullet: 'terraform state rm the adopted resources from snap's infra/shared state') and AC#5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' sub-section: 'If rebar apply fails after state-rm, re-run terraform import on snap's infra/shared state for each removed resource using the same resource addresses. Perform state-rm only after `terraform apply -target` of each resource group succeeds and plan shows clean.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: Destructive/irreversible operation(s) with no stated safeguard.
findings.suggested_fix: State the safeguard (backup, dry-run, reversible migration, rollback) or justify the irreversibility explicitly (T4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a safe import procedure for the two `random_password` live-value imports: e.g., read the value from SSM into an env-var (`TF_VAR_x=$(aws ssm get-parameter ...)`) and pass it only via environment, or use a `-var-file` written to tmpfs and shredded after import; confirm no echo/logging of the value.
findings.finding: The plan instructs reading the live `random_password` values from SSM / the Lambda environment and passing them into `terraform import`, but specifies no safe injection method. Passing a secret value on the CLI exposes it in shell history, process-list (`ps aux`), and potentially in CI logs — a plaintext-secret-in-logs risk on the boundary-crossing auth path (T5c secret lifecycle dimension).
findings.location: Plan body — 'import the two random_password resources with their LIVE values (read from SSM / the Lambda env)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the operator runbook section, specify: `export TF_VAR_cookie_signing_secret=$(aws ssm get-parameter --name /snap-demo/... --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text)` then `terraform import ...` — env-var injection avoids shell-history exposure. Shred the var after import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a recovery procedure for partial-import failure: specify which resources are safe to re-import, how to detect partial state (e.g., `terraform state list` check before apply), and a rollback path (e.g., `terraform state rm` the partial imports to restore snap as sole owner).
findings.finding: Partial `terraform import` failure (e.g., after 10 of 18 resources) leaves rebar state inconsistently populated with no rollback path or recovery procedure described, risking a corrupted state that neither rebar nor snap fully owns.
findings.location: ## What — `terraform import` each live resource

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an inline HCL comment on `legacy_name_prefix` (and/or an AC requiring it) explaining that it must match the original snap-demo physical names to avoid resource recreation, and noting the condition under which it can be removed.
findings.finding: The `legacy_name_prefix = "snap-demo"` local is a safety-critical, non-obvious artifact: changing it would silently trigger destruction and recreation of 18 live resources, but the plan includes no in-code comment, ADR note, or design-doc entry explaining its purpose, constraints, or conditions under which it may eventually be retired.
findings.location: What section — `legacy_name_prefix = "snap-demo"` local; also Scope/AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the minimum IAM actions and resource ARNs for the Lambda execution role in `auth_host.tf` (e.g. `ssm:GetParameter` scoped to specific parameter paths, `logs:CreateLogGroup/PutLogEvents` scoped to the Lambda's log group ARN).
findings.finding: The plan lists 'Lambda + IAM role/policy' for auth_host.tf but never specifies what permissions the Lambda execution role requires. Without a stated permission scope, there is no way to verify least-privilege and the implementation could default to an overly broad policy.
findings.location: What section — 'New infra/terraform/auth_host.tf: Lambda + IAM role/policy + API GW v2…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `aws_iam_policy_document` block in the plan for the Lambda role listing only: `logs:CreateLogGroup`, `logs:CreateLogStream`, `logs:PutLogEvents` on the Lambda's own log group ARN, plus `ssm:GetParameter`/`kms:Decrypt` scoped to the specific SSM parameter paths the auth-host function reads.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or inline note in `auth_host.tf` spec that the Lambda execution role is scoped to the minimum required actions (e.g., `ssm:GetParameter` on `/rebar/prod/auth-host/*` only, no wildcard) and that the adopted role's policy is audited and tightened during adoption.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a Lambda IAM role/policy for the auth_host Lambda (an internet-reachable component) but gives no bounds on what the Lambda execution role is granted — no resource ARN scope, no action list, no condition constraints are described. For a boundary-crossing component, an unspecified privilege grant is a T5c least-privilege gap.
findings.location: Plan body — 'New infra/terraform/auth_host.tf: Lambda + IAM role/policy'
findings.suggested_fix: In the auth_host.tf spec bullet, append: 'Lambda execution role grants only ssm:GetParameter on /rebar/prod/auth-cookie-signing-* and logs:CreateLogGroup/CreateLogStream/PutLogEvents on its own log group ARN — no wildcards on resource or action.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to the `aws_acm_certificate` resource in `acm.tf` and to the `aws_cloudfront_distribution` in `auth_host.tf`, matching the precedent set for `aws_ebs_volume.data` and `aws_eip.gerrit`.
findings.finding: The wildcard ACM certificate (`*.solutions.navateam.com`) and CloudFront distribution are shared, production-critical resources. The plan specifies no `prevent_destroy` lifecycle guard on either, meaning a misconfigured future apply or `terraform destroy` could drop TLS for all subdomains (including the Gerrit instance at `rebar.solutions.navateam.com`) and take down the public CloudFront auth endpoint.
findings.location: What section — 'New infra/terraform/acm.tf: wildcard cert…' and 'New infra/terraform/auth_host.tf: … CloudFront distribution + Route53 A/AAAA'
findings.suggested_fix: In acm.tf: add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to `aws_acm_certificate.wildcard`. In auth_host.tf: add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to `aws_cloudfront_distribution.auth_host`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC#2) to explicitly verify the Lambda source files (`index.js`, `cookie.js`) are present at the declared paths and that `terraform plan` produces a valid archive (evidence: plan output showing `archive_file` data source with non-zero size).
findings.finding: The Lambda source file copy (`infra/terraform/auth/auth-host/index.js` and `auth/lib/cookie.js`) is a named deliverable in 'What' but has no acceptance criterion that explicitly verifies these files exist and are correct; AC#3 only implicitly covers them via `terraform validate`.
findings.location: What ('Copy Lambda source infra/terraform/auth/auth-host/index.js + auth/lib/cookie.js'); AC#3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before closing this ticket, enumerate (in the PR description or a comment) the specific existing docs/runbooks/CLAUDE.md sections that reference snap ownership of the 18 migrated resources, so the follow-on doc child can target them explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan defers all documentation to a 'separate child' but does not identify or schedule remediation of existing stale references — specifically, any existing docs, runbooks, or CLAUDE.md entries that reference snap as the owner of the GitHub OIDC provider, the wildcard cert, or the auth_host SSO stack will be stranded after this migration.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Docs are a separate child'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a coordination step to prevent snap CI from running during the dual-ownership window: e.g., disable snap's infra/shared pipeline, lock snap's remote state (S3 + DynamoDB lock), or document the exact sequence with a mandatory human gate before state rm.
findings.finding: During the window between `terraform import` into rebar state and `terraform state rm` from snap's state, both states reference the same live AWS resources; if snap's CI pipeline runs during this window it can apply changes (or destroy) resources that rebar now also owns, with no coordination mechanism specified.
findings.location: ## What — `terraform import` / `terraform state rm` steps

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-apply validation step to the AC: a smoke test of the auth_host SSO endpoint (e.g., curl the auth_host CloudFront domain for a 302/200), and confirm existing CloudWatch alarms (or add one) cover Lambda errors and API GW 5xx rates.
findings.finding: No observability or health validation is specified post-migration for the auth_host Lambda, API Gateway, and CloudFront distribution; operators have no defined signal to confirm the SSO stack is healthy after retag/apply.
findings.location: AC: `terraform apply` completed; ## What — auth_host.tf

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document that the SSM parameter and Lambda env variable must be frozen (no rotation) during the read-import window, and specify the exact CLI commands to atomically read the live value and immediately run `terraform import` in the same shell session to minimize the race window.
findings.finding: Reading `random_password` live values from SSM / Lambda env and then importing them into terraform state is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) race: if the secret is rotated or the SSM parameter is updated between the read and the import, the value in terraform state diverges from the live value, and the next `terraform plan` will show a secret-value change (violating AC) or silently rotate the secret on apply.
findings.location: ## What — 'import the two `random_password` resources with their LIVE values (read from SSM / the Lambda env)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step (or explicit deferral note) for removing/commenting out the 18 adopted resources from snap's `infra/shared` HCL after `terraform state rm`, so snap's terraform does not diverge and fail on its next plan/apply.
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge that snap's `infra/shared` HCL configuration still contains definitions for the 18 adopted resources after `terraform state rm`. Removing resources from snap's state without removing or nullifying their HCL means snap's next `terraform plan` will show all 18 resources as 'to be created', which would either fail (resource already exists) or accidentally recreate them — silently breaking the migration.
findings.location: ## What — last bullet (`terraform state rm` the adopted resources from snap's `infra/shared` state)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under ## What: 'After `state rm`, remove (or replace with `removed {}` blocks in TF ≥1.7) the HCL definitions for the 18 adopted resources from snap's `infra/shared` terraform, and verify `terraform plan` on snap's side shows no unexpected creates.' If this is explicitly owned by a separate ticket, note that in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the `archive_file`-packaged Lambda ZIP (from the copied `index.js` + `cookie.js`) produces a SHA256 hash identical to the live deployed function's `source_code_hash` before committing to the `archive_file` approach, or document that a Lambda code update is acceptable and intentional.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `archive_file` will package the Lambda source (`index.js` + `cookie.js`) and that after import `terraform plan` will show no replace. This is an unverified capability assumption: `archive_file` recomputes the ZIP hash from local source on every plan. If the live Lambda was deployed from a ZIP built by snap's pipeline (possibly with different file permissions, ordering, or a build step), the computed hash will differ from the deployed function's `source_code_hash`, triggering a Lambda update on first `apply` — which is a functional change to a live auth endpoint, not just a retag.
findings.location: `What` section — `auth_host.tf`: `archive_file` + Lambda; AC line 4: 'terraform plan shows no destroy/replace and no secret-value change'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `random_password` import with a caller-supplied plaintext value is supported by the random provider version in snap's state (requires ≥3.6.0), and document the exact import ID format and fallback procedure if unsupported.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `terraform import` of two `random_password` resources 'with their LIVE values (read from SSM / the Lambda env) so no secret rotates.' Import support for `random_password` was added in hashicorp/random v3.6.0, and the import ID must be the plaintext secret value itself. This is an unverified, version-gated capability: if snap's random provider version is <3.6.0 the import subcommand will reject the resource type entirely, and the plan provides no fallback (e.g., `terraform state push` with a crafted state JSON, or accepting a one-time rotation).
findings.location: What section: '`terraform import` each live resource; import the two `random_password` resources with their LIVE values'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace manual CLI `terraform import` commands with declarative HCL `import { }` blocks (Terraform ≥ 1.5, already satisfied by `versions.tf`), so the 18 adoptions are captured in the PR diff, are idempotent, and are visible to `terraform plan`.
findings.finding: The plan uses manual CLI `terraform import` commands rather than Terraform 1.5+ declarative `import` blocks. Since `versions.tf` already requires Terraform ≥ 1.10, import blocks are available. CLI-based imports are not captured in the PR diff, are not idempotent (re-running errors if the resource is already in state), and cannot be validated by `terraform plan` in CI — creating a reproducibility and auditability gap for the 18 resources being adopted.
findings.location: What section — 'terraform import each live resource; import the two random_password resources with their LIVE values'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the correct `thumbprint_list` value for `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github` against AWS's current guidance (which now auto-manages this for GitHub's OIDC endpoint) and either use an empty list, use `data "tls_certificate"` dynamic resolution, or confirm the live provider's stored thumbprint matches the hardcoded value before committing.
findings.finding: The plan hardcodes OIDC thumbprint `6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1` for the GitHub Actions provider, but AWS updated its guidance (~2023) to state that thumbprints for GitHub's OIDC endpoint are now managed automatically by AWS — the recommended approach is either an empty thumbprint list or dynamic resolution via `data "tls_certificate"`. Using the stale hardcoded value can cause perpetual `terraform plan` drift if AWS's stored thumbprint list differs, and is known prior art the plan should address.
findings.location: What section — `oidc.tf` spec: `thumbprint = '6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1'`
findings.suggested_fix: Use `data "tls_certificate" "github_actions" { url = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com/.well-known/openid-configuration" }` and set `thumbprint_list = [data.tls_certificate.github_actions.certificates[0].sha1_fingerprint]`, or check AWS docs — for GitHub specifically, `thumbprint_list = []` is now accepted and AWS manages it automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement in the plan (or as a comment in `auth_host.tf`) naming the human authentication mechanism for the auth_host endpoint (e.g. 'end-users authenticate via Google OAuth 2.0 OIDC; the Lambda verifies the Google ID token and sets a signed cookie; unauthenticated requests are redirected to Google login').
findings.finding: The plan creates a public CloudFront distribution + API GW v2 endpoint for the auth_host SSO stack but does not explicitly name the human authentication mechanism for end-users (and administrators) reaching this service. 'SSO' and 'Google-client-secret' are mentioned only as implementation details for `auth_sso.tf` locals, not as a stated access contract for the deployed endpoint.
findings.location: What section — 'New infra/terraform/auth_sso.tf: … Google-client-secret data source + SSO locals' and 'New infra/terraform/auth_host.tf: … CloudFront distribution + Route53 A/AAAA'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` for the auth-host Lambda in `auth_host.tf`, and add at least one CloudWatch alarm (e.g. on Lambda error rate or API GW 5XX count) wired to the existing `aws_sns_topic.alerts` SNS topic.
findings.finding: The plan adds a Lambda function and CloudFront distribution but specifies no CloudWatch log group, access logging, or alarms for the new auth_host infrastructure. If the Lambda errors out or the auth flow silently breaks, there is no observability path.
findings.location: What section — 'New infra/terraform/auth_host.tf: Lambda + IAM role/policy + API GW v2 (api/integration/route/stage) + lambda permission + CloudFront distribution + Route53 A/AAAA + origin secret'
findings.suggested_fix: In auth_host.tf: declare `resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "auth_host" { name = "/aws/lambda/${local.legacy_name_prefix}-auth-host"; retention_in_days = 30 }` and `resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "auth_host_errors" { ... namespace = "AWS/Lambda"; metric_name = "Errors"; alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn] }`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 'Security contract' paragraph to the plan (or an AC) declaring the access posture of each auth_host public endpoint: state whether CloudFront is the sole enforced entry point, whether API GW v2 has an authorizer or enforces the origin-secret header, and whether the Lambda has no direct function URL; mark the surface public-with-rationale (SSO login flow) + authenticated internally.
findings.finding: The auth_host SSO stack (CloudFront distribution, API GW v2, Lambda) is a public-internet-reachable surface but its access posture is never declared anywhere in the plan. The plan does not state whether API GW v2 has an authorizer, whether the Lambda has a function URL, whether CloudFront is the sole enforced entry point, or whether the auth flow is public-with-rationale vs authenticated. An undeclared posture on a boundary-crossing path is itself the T5c failure (OWASP ASVS V1.4 / access control design).
findings.location: Plan body — 'New infra/terraform/auth_host.tf: Lambda + IAM role/policy + API GW v2 (api/integration/route/stage) + lambda permission + CloudFront distribution + Route53 A/AAAA + origin secret'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[security] CloudFront is the sole public entry point; API GW v2 stage enforces the origin-secret header (checked in Lambda handler); no Lambda function URL is enabled; access posture = public-login-flow behind SSO gate.' Reference OWASP ASVS V1.4.1.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
